title: Unit 05 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Personal Finance and Consumer Economics.

choices.text: A plan for how money will be earned, spent, and saved
choices.feedback: Correct. A budget is a plan for managing money.

choices.text: A list of only luxury purchases
choices.feedback: A budget includes more than luxury purchases.

choices.text: Money borrowed from a lender
choices.feedback: That describes credit or a loan, not a budget.

choices.text: A rule used by government to regulate markets
choices.feedback: That is not related to personal budgeting.

questions.prompt: Needs are expenses that are necessary, while wants are expenses that are optional.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Needs are necessary expenses, while wants are optional.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. The distinction between needs and wants is an important part of budgeting.

choices.text: Borrowing money to buy something right away
choices.feedback: That describes borrowing, not saving.

choices.text: Setting money aside for future use
choices.feedback: Correct. Saving means keeping money for future needs or goals.

choices.text: Spending all income as soon as it is earned
choices.feedback: Saving involves setting money aside, not spending it all immediately.

choices.text: Choosing the most expensive product to reduce risk
choices.feedback: That does not define saving.

questions.prompt: What is interest in personal finance?

choices.text: A type of product warranty
choices.feedback: Interest is not a warranty.

choices.text: A fee paid only when buying groceries
choices.feedback: Interest is not limited to groceries or shopping.

choices.text: Money earned on savings or paid on borrowed money
choices.feedback: Correct. Interest can help savings grow or increase the cost of borrowing.

choices.text: A list of monthly expenses in a budget
choices.feedback: That does not define interest.

questions.prompt: Credit allows a person to borrow money now and repay it later.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Credit is the ability to borrow now and repay later.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. That is the basic meaning of credit.

questions.prompt: Which choice best shows responsible use of debt?

choices.text: Borrowing more than can be repaid
choices.feedback: Responsible borrowing means choosing debt that can be repaid.

choices.text: Ignoring the interest rate on a loan
choices.feedback: Understanding interest is part of responsible borrowing.

choices.text: Making payments on time and understanding the full cost of borrowing
choices.feedback: Correct. Responsible debt use includes timely repayment and awareness of total cost.

choices.text: Using debt for every purchase without a plan
choices.feedback: Borrowing without a plan is not financially responsible.

questions.prompt: Why is it important to compare purchase options before buying?

choices.text: To choose based only on the lowest price
choices.feedback: Price matters, but it is not the only factor.

choices.text: To avoid thinking about quality or long-term value
choices.feedback: Good consumer decisions consider quality and long-term value.

choices.text: To reduce financial risk and find the choice that best fits needs and budget
choices.feedback: Correct. Comparing options helps consumers make more responsible financial choices.

choices.text: To make sure all purchases take the same amount of time
choices.feedback: Time is not the main reason consumers compare options.

questions.prompt: Investing usually involves more risk than keeping money in a basic savings account.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Investing generally has more risk, but it may also offer greater growth over time.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. Basic saving is usually lower risk than investing.
